Our cities are vital centers of human activity. They are homes for millions of people. They drive innovation, culture, and economic growth. Yet, these dynamic places face a growing and serious threat. This profound threat is climate change. We must urgently make our urban areas resilient. This is not merely an option to consider. It is an absolute necessity for our shared future. The quality of urban life for generations to come depends directly on the actions we take today. We need to safeguard our communities. We must build stronger, safer, and more adaptable places for everyone. Heat waves are becoming far more intense and frequent. Cities with their vast expanses of concrete and asphalt trap heat. This creates what is known as the urban heat island effect. These materials absorb and retain the sun's energy. As a result, temperatures within cities can soar to dangerous, even life-threatening levels. This intense heat places immense stress on residents.

Our most vulnerable populations, including the elderly and children, suffer the most severe consequences. Furthermore, our energy systems strain under the massive demand for air conditioning. We must find effective ways to cool our urban environments. This is absolutely crucial for public health and overall livability in a warming world. Flooding presents another major and escalating danger to our urban centers. Climate change is bringing more intense rainfall events. Storms are becoming more frequent and significantly more severe than in the past. Many older urban drainage systems were not designed for these new extremes. They simply cannot cope with the sheer volume of water. Streets can quickly transform into raging rivers. Homes, businesses, and critical infrastructure become inundated. The damage caused by such events is often devastating. People can lose their property, their livelihoods, and their sense of security. The economic costs are truly staggering.

We need cities that are designed to manage water intelligently and sustainably. Rising sea levels pose an existential threat to our coastal cities. A great many of the world's most important urban centers are located by the ocean. These cities are vital hubs for global trade, culture, and human connection. But the world seas are rising steadily and relentlessly. This gradual encroachment puts everything in these low-lying areas at considerable risk. Homes, schools, hospitals, and essential infrastructure like roads and power grids are in increasing danger. Freshwater sources can become contaminated by saltwater intrusion. Some communities may even face the difficult prospect of displacement. We need courageous and forwardthinking plans to address this. We must protect these valuable coastal areas with determination. Nature itself holds many of the answers we seek. Green infrastructure is a powerful approach. It uses natural systems and processes to build resilience in our cities.

This strategy is about working in harmony with nature, not against it. This intelligent approach offers a multitude of benefits. It can significantly cool our urban areas, mitigating the heat island effect. It can effectively manage storm water, reducing flood risk. It also improves air quality and enhances urban biodiversity. Embracing green infrastructure is a smart and sustainable path forward. It allows for urban development that respects and leverages natural processes. Parks and urban forests are essential components of green infrastructure. These spaces are much more than just areas for recreation. They act as the green lungs for our bustling cities. Trees provide vital shade which naturally cools the surrounding air. They also play a crucial role in absorbing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Urban forests create valuable habitats for wildlife, enriching the city's ecosystem. Importantly, they offer places for people to connect with nature.

This connection is fundamental to our well-being. Investing in parks and urban green spaces is a direct investment in our collective health and happiness. Imagine our buildings adorned with living plants. Green roofs and green walls are innovative ways to integrate to nature. Green roofs are covered with vegetation. They absorb rain water effectively. This helps to reduce storm water runoff and lessen the burden on drainage systems. They also provide excellent insulation for buildings. This in turn saves energy on heating and cooling. Green walls or vertical gardens can cool building surfaces. They also contribute to cleaning the air we breathe. These living layers can truly transform our concrete jungles. They bring nature directly into the densest parts of the urban fabric. Other forms of green infrastructure are also highly effective. Permeable pavements are a great example. These surfaces allow rainwater to soak directly into the ground beneath them.

This helps to recharge vital groundwater supplies. It also significantly reduces surface runoff and the risk of flooding. Bioailes are another clever solution. These are specially designed vegetative channels. They slow down storm water, filter pollutants, and allow water to infiltrate the soil. These are relatively simple but remarkably effective solutions. They help our cities mimic natural water cycles much more closely. Widespread adoption can make a real difference. How we move around our cities profoundly matters. Our current reliance on private cars contributes significantly to air pollution and urban heat. Cars also consume a vast amount of valuable urban space. We urgently need cleaner, more efficient, and more equitable ways to travel. Sustainable transportation is absolutely key to creating resilient and livable cities. It helps reduce our collective carbon footprint. It improves air quality leading to better public health outcomes.

This necessary shift is not just about new technologies. It is about fundamentally redesigning our urban spaces to prioritize people, not just vehicles. A robust and accessible public transport system is essential. Highcapacity options like buses, trains, and trams can move many people very efficiently. This significantly reduces traffic congestion on our roads. It also lowers harmful emissions per person transported. Investing generously in public transport makes cities more accessible for everyone. It connects people to jobs, education, health care, and social opportunities. Reliable, affordable, and extensive public transit is a cornerstone. It underpins a thriving, equitable, and resilient urban future for all residents. It ensures everyone can participate fully in city life. Cities should be designed for people first and foremost. Walkable streets are safer, more pleasant, and more vibrant. They encourage social interaction and support local businesses.

Cycling is a healthy zero emission way to travel. It is good for individuals and for the environment. We need to create more dedicated bike lanes. We need more pedestrian friendly zones and public squares. This encourages active lifestyles among residents. It also reduces our dangerous dependence on fossil fuels. Creating these human-caled environments fosters a stronger sense of community. It makes our cities more enjoyable places to live, work, and play. The integration of new mobility options is also exciting. Electric vehicles are becoming more common, reducing tailpipe emissions. Bike sharing and scooter sharing programs offer flexible last mile solutions. These can effectively complement existing public transport networks. Smart city technologies can help manage these diverse options efficiently. The ultimate goal is to create a seamless multimodal transport system. This system should offer people a variety of convenient choices.

It must make sustainable travel easy, attractive, and the default option. Embracing innovation and mobility is crucial for building the climate resilient cities of tomorrow. Successfully adapting to climate change is not just a job for experts. It requires the active involvement of everyone. Local communities possess invaluable knowledge. They understand their own neighborhoods best. They know the specific challenges and vulnerabilities they face daily. Their direct input is absolutely crucial for developing effective and relevant solutions. Engaging residents in the planning process builds a sense of ownership. It fosters widespread support for necessary changes. This collaborative bottom-up approach ensures that adaptation plans are practical, equitable, and truly reflect the diverse needs of the entire urban population. The nature of climate change is inherently unpredictable. We cannot know with absolute certainty what the future holds.

Therefore, our plans and strategies need to be flexible and dynamic. Adaptive design is an approach that acknowledges this uncertainty. It means creating systems, infrastructure, and policies that can be adjusted over time. It is about continuous learning and responding to new information as it becomes available. This iterative approach helps us build genuine resilience in the face of evolving challenges. It allows our cities to strengthen their defenses as climate impacts become clearer and potentially more severe. Strong leadership and clear supportive policies are vital. Governments at all levels need to prioritize climate resilience in their agendas. They must make significant investments in green infrastructure and sustainable transport. They also need to create robust regulatory frameworks. These frameworks should support community-led action and facilitate adaptive management practices. This requires a long-term vision that transcends short political cycles.

It demands unwavering commitment from our leaders. Effective governance can unlock the necessary resources. It can coordinate the complex efforts needed for transformative change across all sectors of urban life. The challenges posed by climate change are indeed significant. However, our cities have always been centers of human creativity and innovation. We possess the knowledge, the technology, and the ingenuity to rethink urban planning. We can build cities that are not only resilient, but also sustainable, equitable, and truly vibrant. By working together with shared purpose and determination, we can adapt and thrive. Our urban centers can become inspiring beacons of hope in a rapidly changing world. The future of urban living can be incredibly bright. We must embrace this necessary transformation with courage, collaboration, and collective will.

Urban planning is the field specializing in the when, where and how of cities growth and development. There are two main branches of city planning. The first is long range planning and the second is current planning. When people think about urban planning, they often think about long range plans. If you can think about it, cities probably plan for it and urban planners probably have a part in that. But long-range planning is only half of what planners do. And in many cases, it's actually more like 20% of what we do.

Current planning is the actual application of the codes and ordinances set forth by the governing body of your community and guided by the master plans approved and guided through long-range planning. Games like City Skylines and Sim City, while very fun, do give the impression that city planners have a lot more control than they actually do. In reality, city planners are analysts. We review projects, we apply the codes, and we sometimes give recommendations of approval or denial. But at the end of the day, we do not get to decide whether or not a shopping mall goes in next to your home or if a cool new cop shop opens up down the street. The core function of an urban planner is to take the information of a specific project, compare it against the approved codes and ordinances of the city, and then synthesize it into a staff report which is presented to members of the planning commission or city council or other decision-making body. So that way they have a complete formed idea of what the project is, how it fits into approved plans, how it fits into land use assumptions and general planning theory.

Using that information, the governing body can make a recommendation of approval or denial and then decide on what gets built at the end of the day. It's important to note that planners are not the decision makers in this scenario. Only the elected officials are. Planners only serve as an advisory body. We are professionals. We are trained technicians. We are here to provide a unbiased recommendation or analysis depending on the jurisdiction that you live in. Some jurisdictions do not allow planners to make recommendations of approval or denial, but only to provide the facts of the case as known by the planners themselves. In some jurisdictions, planners do get the opportunity to make a recommendation. But again, in no jurisdiction do planners get to make the final decision unless it is through an administrative review process, which is very specific and often for very minor things.

They simply cannot cope with the sheer volume of water. Streets can quickly transform into raging rivers. Homes, businesses, and critical infrastructure become inundated.
The damage caused by such events is often devastating. People can lose their property, their livelihoods, and their sense of security. The economic costs are truly staggering.

We need cities that are designed to manage water intelligently and sustainably. Rising sea levels pose an existential threat to our coastal cities. A great many of the world's most important urban centers are located by the ocean.

These cities are vital hubs for global trade, culture, and human connection. But the world seas are rising steadily and relentlessly. This gradual encroachment puts everything in these low-lying areas at considerable risk.
Homes, schools, hospitals, and essential infrastructure like roads and power grids are in increasing danger. Freshwater sources can become contaminated by saltwater intrusion.
Some communities may even face the difficult prospect of displacement. We need courageous and forwardthinking plans to address this. We must protect these valuable coastal areas with determination.
Nature itself holds many of the answers we seek. Green infrastructure is a powerful approach. It uses natural systems and processes to build resilience in our cities.

This strategy is about working in harmony with nature, not against it. This intelligent approach offers a multitude of benefits. It can significantly cool our urban areas, mitigating the heat island effect.
It can effectively manage storm water, reducing flood risk. It also improves air quality and enhances urban biodiversity. Embracing green infrastructure is a smart and sustainable path forward.
It allows for urban development that respects and leverages natural processes. Parks and urban forests are essential components of green infrastructure.
These spaces are much more than just areas for recreation. They act as the green lungs for our bustling cities. Trees provide vital shade which naturally cools the surrounding air.
They also play a crucial role in absorbing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Urban forests create valuable habitats for wildlife, enriching the city's ecosystem.

Importantly, they offer places for people to connect with nature. This connection is fundamental to our well-being. Investing in parks and urban green spaces is a direct investment in our collective health and happiness.
Imagine our buildings adorned with living plants. Green roofs and green walls are innovative ways to integrate to nature. Green roofs are covered with vegetation.
They absorb rain water effectively. This helps to reduce storm water runoff and lessen the burden on drainage systems. They also provide excellent insulation for buildings.
This in turn saves energy on heating and cooling. Green walls or vertical gardens can cool building surfaces. They also contribute to cleaning the air we breathe.
These living layers can truly transform our concrete jungles. They bring nature directly into the densest parts of the urban fabric. Other forms of green infrastructure are also highly effective.
Permeable pavements are a great example. These surfaces allow rainwater to soak directly into the ground beneath them. This helps to recharge vital groundwater supplies.
It also significantly reduces surface runoff and the risk of flooding. Bioailes are another clever solution. These are specially designed vegetative channels.
They slow down storm water, filter pollutants, and allow water to infiltrate the soil. These are relatively simple but remarkably effective solutions.
They help our cities mimic natural water cycles much more closely. Widespread adoption can make a real difference. How we move around our cities profoundly matters.
Our current reliance on private cars contributes significantly to air pollution and urban heat. Cars also consume a vast amount of valuable urban space.
We urgently need cleaner, more efficient, and more equitable ways to travel. Sustainable transportation is absolutely key to creating resilient and livable cities.
It helps reduce our collective carbon footprint. It improves air quality leading to better public health outcomes. This necessary shift is not just about new technologies.
It is about fundamentally redesigning our urban spaces to prioritize people, not just vehicles. A robust and accessible public transport system is essential.
Highcapacity options like buses, trains, and trams can move many people very efficiently. This significantly reduces traffic congestion on our roads.

It also lowers harmful emissions per person transported. Investing generously in public transport makes cities more accessible for everyone. It connects people to jobs, education, health care, and social opportunities.
Reliable, affordable, and extensive public transit is a cornerstone. It underpins a thriving, equitable, and resilient urban future for all residents.
It ensures everyone can participate fully in city life. Cities should be designed for people first and foremost. Walkable streets are safer, more pleasant, and more vibrant.
They encourage social interaction and support local businesses. Cycling is a healthy zero emission way to travel. It is good for individuals and for the environment.
We need to create more dedicated bike lanes. We need more pedestrian friendly zones and public squares. This encourages active lifestyles among residents.

It also reduces our dangerous dependence on fossil fuels. Creating these human-caled environments fosters a stronger sense of community. It makes our cities more enjoyable places to live, work, and play.

The integration of new mobility options is also exciting. Electric vehicles are becoming more common, reducing tailpipe emissions. Bike sharing and scooter sharing programs offer flexible last mile solutions.

These can effectively complement existing public transport networks. Smart city technologies can help manage these diverse options efficiently. The ultimate goal is to create a seamless multimodal transport system.

This system should offer people a variety of convenient choices. It must make sustainable travel easy, attractive, and the default option. Embracing innovation and mobility is crucial for building the climate resilient cities of tomorrow.
Successfully adapting to climate change is not just a job for experts. It requires the active involvement of everyone. Local communities possess invaluable knowledge.

They understand their own neighborhoods best. They know the specific challenges and vulnerabilities they face daily. Their direct input is absolutely crucial for developing effective and relevant solutions.

Engaging residents in the planning process builds a sense of ownership. It fosters widespread support for necessary changes. This collaborative bottom-up approach ensures that adaptation plans are practical, equitable, and truly reflect the diverse needs of the entire urban population.
The nature of climate change is inherently unpredictable. We cannot know with absolute certainty what the future holds. Therefore, our plans and strategies need to be flexible and dynamic.
Adaptive design is an approach that acknowledges this uncertainty. It means creating systems, infrastructure, and policies that can be adjusted over time.
It is about continuous learning and responding to new information as it becomes available. This iterative approach helps us build genuine resilience in the face of evolving challenges.
It allows our cities to strengthen their defenses as climate impacts become clearer and potentially more severe. Strong leadership and clear supportive policies are vital.
Governments at all levels need to prioritize climate resilience in their agendas. They must make significant investments in green infrastructure and sustainable transport.
They also need to create robust regulatory frameworks. These frameworks should support community-led action and facilitate adaptive management practices.
This requires a long-term vision that transcends short political cycles. It demands unwavering commitment from our leaders. Effective governance can unlock the necessary resources.
It can coordinate the complex efforts needed for transformative change across all sectors of urban life. The challenges posed by climate change are indeed significant.
However, our cities have always been centers of human creativity and innovation. We possess the knowledge, the technology, and the ingenuity to rethink urban planning.
We can build cities that are not only resilient, but also sustainable, equitable, and truly vibrant. By working together with shared purpose and determination, we can adapt and thrive.

Our urban centers can become inspiring beacons of hope in a rapidly changing world. The future of urban living can be incredibly bright. We must embrace this necessary transformation with courage, collaboration, and collective will.

Urban planning is the field specializing in the when, where and how of cities growth and development. There are two main branches of city planning. The first is long range planning and the second is current planning.

When people think about urban planning, they often think about long range plans. These are your comprehensive plans, your land use plans, your housing plans, your utility plans, your thoroughare master plans.
If you can think about it, cities probably plan for it and urban planners probably have a part in that. But long-range planning is only half of what planners do.

And in many cases, it's actually more like 20% of what we do. Current planning is the actual application of the codes and ordinances set forth by the governing body of your community and guided by the master plans approved and guided through long-range planning.
This is more like again 80% of what planners actually do. And it's the more mundane day-to-day stuff. Things like sign permits, special use permits, special event permits, and building permits.

And while it's not super glorious, this is the meat and potatoes of what planning actually is. When it comes time to build a new apartment complex or a shopping center, the current planning is how you actually translate those goals and objectives set in your comprehensive plans into actual construction and products and developments.
So, while it's not super sexy, it is very important. So, now that we've talked a little bit about what urban planning is, let's talk about what it is not.
Games like City Skylines and Sim City, while very fun, do give the impression that city planners have a lot more control than they actually do. In reality, city planners are analysts.

We review projects, we apply the codes, and we sometimes give recommendations of approval or denial. But at the end of the day, we do not get to decide whether or not a shopping mall goes in next to your home or if a cool new cop shop opens up down the street.

The core function of an urban planner is to take the information of a specific project, compare it against the approved codes and ordinances of the city, and then synthesize it into a staff report which is presented to members of the planning commission or city council or other decision-making body.
So that way they have a complete formed idea of what the project is, how it fits into approved plans, how it fits into land use assumptions and general planning theory.

Using that information, the governing body can make a recommendation of approval or denial and then decide on what gets built at the end of the day. It's important to note that planners are not the decision makers in this scenario.

Only the elected officials are. Planners only serve as an advisory body. We are professionals. We are trained technicians. We are here to provide a unbiased recommendation or analysis depending on the jurisdiction that you live in.
Some jurisdictions do not allow planners to make recommendations of approval or denial, but only to provide the facts of the case as known by the planners themselves.

In some jurisdictions, planners do get the opportunity to make a recommendation. But again, in no jurisdiction do planners get to make the final decision unless it is through an administrative review process, which is very specific and often for very minor things.
Another common myth perpetuated by games like City Skylines and Sim Cities is that planners have control over where roads go. In reality, city planners mostly deal with private development projects.

While we have a certain amount of sway with thoroughfare master plans, the majority of road construction actually falls to public works departments. People like city engineers, traffic engineers, and staff engineers handle most of the locating and sighting of new roads and road improvements.
